subject: Physics
q: A leak proof cylinder of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> made of a metal which has very low coefficient of expansion is floating vertically in water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> such that its height above the water surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the temperature of water is increased to <math><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the height of the cylinder above the water surface becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The density of water at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> relative to the density at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is close to:
question: A leak proof cylinder of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> made of a metal which has very low coefficient of expansion is floating vertically in water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> such that its height above the water surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When the temperature of water is increased to <math><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the height of the cylinder above the water surface becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The density of water at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> relative to the density at <math><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is close to:
solution:
According to Archemedes Principle, weight of the body is balanced by the upward thrust force due to buoyancy.

At 0°C, mg=A(100-20)ρ0°Cg, where A is area of cross-section of cylinder and ρ0°C is density of water at 0°C.
At 4°C, mg=A(100-21)ρ4°Cg, where ρ4°C is density of water at 4°C.
ρ4°Cρ0°C=8079=1.01

subject: Physics
q:
A spherical ball of radius $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$ and density $10^5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ falls freely under gravity through a distance $h$ before entering a tank of water, If after entering in water the velocity of the ball does not change, then the value of $h$ is approximately:
(The coefficient of viscosity of water is $9.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{m}^2$ )
question:
A spherical ball of radius $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}$ and density $10^5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ falls freely under gravity through a distance $h$ before entering a tank of water, If after entering in water the velocity of the ball does not change, then the value of $h$ is approximately:
(The coefficient of viscosity of water is $9.8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~s} / \mathrm{m}^2$ )
solution:
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~g}}{9} \frac{\mathrm{R}^2\left[\rho_{\mathrm{B}}-\rho_{\mathrm{L}}\right]}{\eta} \\& \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{2}{9} \times \frac{10 \times\left(10^{-4}\right)^2}{9.8 \times 10^{-6}}\left[10^5-10^3\right] \\& \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{T}}=224.5\end{aligned}$
when ball fall from height (h)
$\begin{aligned}& \mathrm{V}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}} \\
& \mathrm{h}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}^2}{2 \mathrm{~g}}\right)=2518 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical vessel of cross-section A contains water to a height $\mathrm{h}$. There is a hole in the bottom of radius ' $a$ '. The time in which it will be emptied is:
question: A cylindrical vessel of cross-section A contains water to a height $\mathrm{h}$. There is a hole in the bottom of radius ' $a$ '. The time in which it will be emptied is:
solution:
$$ \text { Let the rate of falling water level be }-\frac{d h}{d t} $$
Initially at $t=0 ; h=h$ $$ t=t ; h=0 $$ Then, $A\left(-\frac{d h}{d t}\right)=\pi a^2 \cdot v$ $$ d t=-\frac{A}{\pi a^2 \sqrt{2 g h}} d h $$ $[\because$ velocity of efflux of liquid $v=\sqrt{2 g h}]$ Integrating both sides $$ \int_0^t d t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \int_h^0 h^{-1 / 2} d h $$ $$ [t]_0^t=-\frac{A}{\sqrt{2 g} \pi a^2} \cdot\left[\frac{h^{1 / 2}}{1 / 2}\right]_h^0 $$ $$ t=\frac{\sqrt{2} A}{\pi a^2} \sqrt{\frac{h}{g}} $$

subject: Physics
q:
Consider following statements:
A. Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the interior as compared to the molecules at the surface, of a liquid.
B. As the temperature of liquid rises, the coefficient of viscosity increases.
C. As the temperature of gas increases, the coefficient of viscosity increases
D. The onset of turbulence is determined by Reynold's number.
E. In a steady flow two stream lines never intersect.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Consider following statements:
A. Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the interior as compared to the molecules at the surface, of a liquid.
B. As the temperature of liquid rises, the coefficient of viscosity increases.
C. As the temperature of gas increases, the coefficient of viscosity increases
D. The onset of turbulence is determined by Reynold's number.
E. In a steady flow two stream lines never intersect.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution: Surface tension arises due to extra energy of the molecules at the molecules at the surface as compared at the interior of a liquid. The coefficient of viscosity for a liquid decreases with rise in temperature where as it increases for gases with increase in temperature. The flow is turbulent for a Reynold's number greater than 2000. Stream lines never intersect in a steady flow.

q: A solid sphere, of radius R acquires a terminal velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> when falling (due to gravity) through a viscous fluid having a coefficient of viscosity <math><mi>η</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The sphere is broken into 27 identical solid spheres. If each of these spheres acquires a terminal velocity, <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> when falling through the same fluid, the ratio <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> equals:
question: A solid sphere, of radius R acquires a terminal velocity <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> when falling (due to gravity) through a viscous fluid having a coefficient of viscosity <math><mi>η</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The sphere is broken into 27 identical solid spheres. If each of these spheres acquires a terminal velocity, <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> when falling through the same fluid, the ratio <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> equals:
solution:
If R is the radius of initial drop and r is the radius of new drop, then
43πR3=27×43πr3⇒r=R3
Terminal velocity,

         vT∝r2
⇒ v1v2=R2R32=9

subject: Physics
q: Small water droplets of radius $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$ are formed in the upper atmosphere and falling with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Due to condensation, if 8 such droplets are coalesced and formed a larger drop, the new terminal velocity will be _____$\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: Small water droplets of radius $0.01 \mathrm{~mm}$ are formed in the upper atmosphere and falling with a terminal velocity of $10 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. Due to condensation, if 8 such droplets are coalesced and formed a larger drop, the new terminal velocity will be _____$\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
$\mathrm{m}=$ mass of small drop
$\mathrm{M}=$ mass of bigger drop
$\begin{aligned} & V_t=\frac{2}{9} \frac{R^2(\rho-\sigma) g}{\eta} \\ & 8 \propto m=M \\ & 8 r^3=R^3 \Rightarrow R=2 R\end{aligned}$
as $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}} \times \mathrm{R}^2 \quad \because$ Radius double so $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{t}}$ becomes 4 time
$\therefore 4 \times 10=40 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Physics
q: A submarine experiences a pressure of <math><mn>5.05</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> at a depth of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> in a sea. When it goes further to a depth of <math><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , it experiences a pressure of <math><mi> </mi><mn>8.08</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> . Then <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately (density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></math> and acceleration due to gravity <math><mi> </mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
question: A submarine experiences a pressure of <math><mn>5.05</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> at a depth of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> in a sea. When it goes further to a depth of <math><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> , it experiences a pressure of <math><mi> </mi><mn>8.08</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> . Then <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is approximately (density of water <math><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></math> and acceleration due to gravity <math><mi> </mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ):
solution:
The pressure at a depth d is p=p0+ρgd, here p0 is the atmospheric pressure.
∴p1=p0+ρgd1
and p2=p0+ρgd2
The difference in pressure is p2-p1=ρgd2-d1

Putting the values in the above equation
8.08×106-5.05×106=103×10d2-d1
d2-d1=3.03×106104
=303 m

subject: Physics
q: When a long glass capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>015</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> is dipped in a liquid, the liquid rises to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> within it. If the contact angle between the liquid and glass to close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the surface tension of the liquid, in milliNewton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mtext>(liqued) </mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> (Given answer in closed integer)
question: When a long glass capillary tube of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>015</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> is dipped in a liquid, the liquid rises to a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> within it. If the contact angle between the liquid and glass to close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, the surface tension of the liquid, in milliNewton <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mtext>(liqued) </mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>900</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> (Given answer in closed integer)
solution:
Using the concept of rise due to surface tension,

 2Tr=hρg

T=rhρg2=15×10-5×15×10-2×900×102=101 milliNewton m-1

subject: Physics
q: Assume that a drop of a liquid evaporates by a decrease in its surface energy so that its temperature remains unchanged. The minimum radius of the drop for this to be possible is. (The surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, the density of the liquid is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is its latent heat of vaporisation.)
question: Assume that a drop of a liquid evaporates by a decrease in its surface energy so that its temperature remains unchanged. The minimum radius of the drop for this to be possible is. (The surface tension is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, the density of the liquid is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> ρ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is its latent heat of vaporisation.)
solution:
Let the radius of the drop at time t=t be r and at an instant t=t+dt be r-dr

As surface area is given by, A=4πr2,

∴ decrease in surface area is,

dA=4π2rdr

∴ Decrease in surface energy during time dt is,

dU=TdA=T·8πrdr     ...1

Decrease in volume during time dt is,

dV=4πr2dr

∴ Decrease in mass during time dt is,

dm=ρdV=4πρr2dr

∴ Heat required in vaporisation is,

dQ=Ldm=4πρr2drL    ...2

Apply conservation of energy,

decrease in surface energy=heat required in vaporisation.

⇒ dU=dQ

⇒ 8Tπrdr=4πρr2drL

⇒2T=ρrL

⇒r=2TρL

Hence, the minimum radius is 2TρL

subject: Physics
q: Wax is coated on the inner wall of a capillary tube and the tube is then dipped in water. Then, compared to the unwaxed capillary, the angle of contact $\theta$ and the height $h$ upto which water rises change. These changes are :
question: Wax is coated on the inner wall of a capillary tube and the tube is then dipped in water. Then, compared to the unwaxed capillary, the angle of contact $\theta$ and the height $h$ upto which water rises change. These changes are :
solution:
Angle of $\operatorname{contact} \theta$
$$
\cos \theta=\frac{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SA}}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SL}}}{\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{LA}}}
$$
when water is on a waxy or oily surface
$\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SA}} < \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{SL}} \cos \theta$ is negative i.e.,
$$
90^{\circ} < \theta < 180^{\circ}
$$
i.e., angle of contact $\theta$ increases
And for $\theta>90^{\circ}$ liquid level in capillary
tube fall. i.e., h decreases

subject: Physics
q: The height of liquid column raised in a capillary tube of certain radius when dipped in liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> vertically is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the tube is dipped in a similar manner in another liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of surface tension and density double the values of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the height of liquid column raised in liquid B would be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The height of liquid column raised in a capillary tube of certain radius when dipped in liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> vertically is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the tube is dipped in a similar manner in another liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of surface tension and density double the values of liquid <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, the height of liquid column raised in liquid B would be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
The height h through which a liquid will rise in a capillary tube of radius r is given by,

h=2Tcosθrρg∝Tρ∝2T2ρ

As we can see in both cases, value of height will remain same. Therefore,

h'=h=5 cm= 0.05 m

subject: Physics
q: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small identical drops of water. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> be the total surface energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small drops of water and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the surface energy of single big drop of water, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>________.
question: A big drop is formed by coalescing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small identical drops of water. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> be the total surface energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> small drops of water and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the surface energy of single big drop of water, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>________.
solution:
Let radius of the small drop be r and the radius of the big drop be R. Then, total volume will remain constant, therefore

1000×ρ43πr3=43πR3ρ

⇒R=10r

Initial total surface energy, E1=1000×4πr2×S and 

E2=4π(10r)2 S

⇒E1E2=101Hence, x=10.

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement- 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statetnents.
Statement-1: A capillary is dipped in a liquid and liquid rises to a height $h$ in it. As the temperature of the liquid is raised, the height $h$ increases (if the density of the liquid and the angle of contact remain the same).
Statement-2: Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in its temperature.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement- 2 . Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statetnents.
Statement-1: A capillary is dipped in a liquid and liquid rises to a height $h$ in it. As the temperature of the liquid is raised, the height $h$ increases (if the density of the liquid and the angle of contact remain the same).
Statement-2: Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in its temperature.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not the correct explanation for Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is the correct explanation for Statement-1.
solution:
Surface tension of a liquid decreases with the rise in temperture. At the boiling point of liquid, surface tension is zero.
Capillary rise $h=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\mathrm{rdg}}$
As surface tension $\mathrm{T}$ decreases with rise in temperature hence capillary rise also decreases.

subject: Physics
q: A small soap bubble of radius 4cm is trapped inside another bubble of radius 6cm without any contact. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the pressure inside the inner bubble and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the pressure outside the outer bubble. Radius of another bubble with pressure difference <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> between its inside and outside would be:
question: A small soap bubble of radius 4cm is trapped inside another bubble of radius 6cm without any contact. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the pressure inside the inner bubble and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the pressure outside the outer bubble. Radius of another bubble with pressure difference <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> between its inside and outside would be:
solution:
P2−P1=4Tr1      ....(1)P1−P0=4Tr2  .....(2)adding (1) & (2)P2−P0=4Tr1+4Tr2P2=P0+4Tr1+4Tr2P2=P0+4T6+4T4

for another bubble of radius r

P2= P0+4Tr

⇒   1r=16+14

⇒   r=2.4 cm

q: In a test experiment on a model aeroplane in wind tunnel, the flow speeds on the upper and lower surfaces of the wings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. If the wing area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, the lift of the wing is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given density of air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: In a test experiment on a model aeroplane in wind tunnel, the flow speeds on the upper and lower surfaces of the wings are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>70</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>65</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. If the wing area is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, the lift of the wing is _______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. (Given density of air <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Applying Bernoulli's principle on upper & lower surfaces, we get

P1+12ρv12+ρgh1=P2+12ρv22+ρgh2

Since, height difference between upper and lower surface is negligible.

⇒P2-P1=12ρv12-12ρv22

Therefore, upthrust acting due to pressure difference:

⇒FA=12ρv12-v22⇒F=12×1.2×702-652×2

=810 N

subject: Physics
q: A liquid of density $600$ kg/m$^3$ flowing steadily in a tube of varying cross-section. The cross-section at a point $A$ is $1.0$ cm$^2$ and that at $B$ is $20$ mm$^2$. Both the points $A$ and $B$ are in same horizontal plane, the speed of the liquid at $A$ is $10$ cm/s. The difference in pressures at $A$ and $B$ points is ________ Pa.
question: A liquid of density $600$ kg/m$^3$ flowing steadily in a tube of varying cross-section. The cross-section at a point $A$ is $1.0$ cm$^2$ and that at $B$ is $20$ mm$^2$. Both the points $A$ and $B$ are in same horizontal plane, the speed of the liquid at $A$ is $10$ cm/s. The difference in pressures at $A$ and $B$ points is ________ Pa.
solution:
Using the equation of continuity:

$A_A v_A = A_B v_B$

$(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.1 = (20 \times 10^{-6}) \times v_B$

$v_B = \dfrac{10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.5$ m/s

Using Bernoulli's equation for a horizontal tube:

$P_A + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_A^2 = P_B + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v_B^2$

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho (v_B^2 - v_A^2)$

Substituting the given values:

$P_A - P_B = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 600 \times (0.5^2 - 0.1^2)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times (0.25 - 0.01)$

$P_A - P_B = 300 \times 0.24 = 72$ Pa

Answer: $72$

subject: Physics
q: Surface tension of a soap bubble is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Work done to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be : [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math> ]
question: Surface tension of a soap bubble is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . Work done to increase the radius of soap bubble from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> will be : [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>22</mn><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math> ]
solution:
As we know,

Work done = change in surface energy of soap bubble

Therefore,

Work done =2T4πr22-4πr12

=8πTr22-r12

=8×227×0.0272-3.52×10-4 J

=18.48×10-4J

subject: Physics
q: An air bubble of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> rises from the bottom of a lake <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> deep to the surface at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The atmospheric pressure is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the density of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo></math>There is no difference of the temperature of water at the depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and on the surface. The volume of air bubble when it reaches the surface will be
question: An air bubble of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> rises from the bottom of a lake <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> deep to the surface at a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The atmospheric pressure is  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the density of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo></math>There is no difference of the temperature of water at the depth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and on the surface. The volume of air bubble when it reaches the surface will be
solution:
The pressure at the bottom of the lake can be calculated as follows:

Pbottom= P0 + ρgh=105+1000 × 10 × 40 Pa=5×105 Pa 

As the temperature of water remains constant, it can be written that

PbottomVbottom=PtopVtop   ...(1)

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required volume of the bubble when it reaches the surface.

5×105×1=1×105×Vtop⇒Vtop=5×105×11×105 cm3= 5 cm3

subject: Physics
q: A spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is formed inside another spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the internal pressure of the smaller bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in the above system is equal to the internal pressure of the another single soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is _____
question: A spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is formed inside another spherical soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the internal pressure of the smaller bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> in the above system is equal to the internal pressure of the another single soap bubble of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is _____
solution:
Pressure inside a bubble is given by, P0+4Tr.

Pressure inside the bubble of smaller radius will be, 

P2-P1=P0+4T6+4T3

Pressure inside the bubble with radius r, P0+4Tr

Now,

⇒P0+4T6+4T3=P0+4Tr

⇒1r=16+13

⇒r=2 cm

subject: Physics
q: A hydraulic press can lift <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> when a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on the smaller piston. It can lift kg when the diameter of the larger piston is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times and that of the smaller piston is decreased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times keeping the same mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> on the smaller piston.
question: A hydraulic press can lift <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> when a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on the smaller piston. It can lift kg when the diameter of the larger piston is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times and that of the smaller piston is decreased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times keeping the same mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> on the smaller piston.
solution:
Using Pascals law, 

100×gA2=mgA1         ...1

Let m mass can lift M0 in second case then

M0g16A2=mgA1/16          ...2

Since A=πd24

From equation 1 and 2 we get

M016.100=16

⇒M0=25600 kg

subject: Physics
q: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
question: Two cylindrical vessels of equal cross sectional area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ contain water up to height 10 m and 6 m , respectively. If the vessels are connected at their bottom then the work done by the force of gravity is : (Density of water is $10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$ and $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}_1=(\rho \mathrm{A} \times 10) \mathrm{g} \times 5+(\rho \mathrm{A} 6) \mathrm{g} \times 3 \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=\rho \mathrm{Ag}(50+18) \\ & \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=68 \rho \mathrm{Ag}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & U_f=(\rho A \times 16) g \times 4 \\ & =(\rho A g) \times 64\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta U=4 \times \rho A g \\ & =4 \times 1000 \times 2 \times 10=8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A soap bubble is blown to a diameter of $7 \mathrm{~cm} .36960 \mathrm{erg}$ of work is done in blowing it further. If surface tension of soap solution is $40 \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}$ then the new radius is______ $\mathrm{cm}$ Take $\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
question: A soap bubble is blown to a diameter of $7 \mathrm{~cm} .36960 \mathrm{erg}$ of work is done in blowing it further. If surface tension of soap solution is $40 \mathrm{dyne} / \mathrm{cm}$ then the new radius is______ $\mathrm{cm}$ Take $\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right)$
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \omega=\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{S} \Delta \mathrm{A} \\ & 36960 \mathrm{erg}=\frac{40 \text { dyne }}{\mathrm{cm}} 8 \pi\left[(\mathrm{r})^2-\left(\frac{7}{2}\right)^2\right] \mathrm{cm}^2 \\ & \mathrm{r}=7 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : When a capillary tube is dipped into a liquid, the liquid neither rises nor falls in the capillary. The contact angle may be $0^{\circ}$.
Statement II : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : When a capillary tube is dipped into a liquid, the liquid neither rises nor falls in the capillary. The contact angle may be $0^{\circ}$.
Statement II : The contact angle between a solid and a liquid is a property of the material of the solid and liquid as well.
In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true and Statement II is false
solution:
Capillary rise
$\mathrm{h}=\frac{2 \mathrm{~T} \cos \theta}{\rho \mathrm{gr}} \text {; }$
If $\theta=0^{\circ}$ then rise is non-zero
$\therefore$ Statement-1 is incorrect.

subject: Physics
q: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble is thrice the excess pressure inside a second soap bubble. The ratio between the volume of the first and the second bubble is:
question: The excess pressure inside a soap bubble is thrice the excess pressure inside a second soap bubble. The ratio between the volume of the first and the second bubble is:
solution: $\mathrm{P}_1-\mathrm{P}_0=\frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_1} \qquad \quad \quad \quad\mathrm{P}_2-\mathrm{P}_0=\frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_2}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}_1-\mathrm{P}_0=3\left(\mathrm{P}_2-\mathrm{P}_0\right) \\ & \frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_1}=3 \frac{4 \mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{r}_2} \\ & \mathrm{r}_2=3 \mathrm{r}_1 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_1^3}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}_2^3}=\frac{1}{27}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A mercury drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn></math> equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The gain in surface energy is:
question: A mercury drop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is broken into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn></math> equal size droplets. Surface tension of mercury is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The gain in surface energy is:
solution:
As the volume of the mercury will remain same, therefore

43πR3=12543πr3⇒R=5r

Now, increase in surface energy

∆U=125×S×4πr2-S×4πR2

 =0.45×4π×10-3212525-1

=4×0.45×4π×10-6

=2.26×10-5J

subject: Physics
q: Consider a water tank as shown in the figure. It's cross-sectional area is 0.4 m2. The tank has an opening B near the bottom whose cross-section area is 1 cm2. A load of 24 kg is applied on the water at the top when the height of the water level is 40 cm above the bottom, the velocity of water coming out the opening B is v m s-1. The value of v, to the nearest integer, is ___ .[Take the value of g to be 10 m s-2]
question: Consider a water tank as shown in the figure. It's cross-sectional area is 0.4 m2. The tank has an opening B near the bottom whose cross-section area is 1 cm2. A load of 24 kg is applied on the water at the top when the height of the water level is 40 cm above the bottom, the velocity of water coming out the opening B is v m s-1. The value of v, to the nearest integer, is ___ .[Take the value of g to be 10 m s-2]
solution:
m=24 kg

A=0.4 m2

a=1 cm2

H=40 cm

Using Bernoulli's equation, 

⇒P0+mgA+ρgH+12Pv12

=P0+0+12ρv2   ...1

⇒ Neglecting v1

⇒v=2gH+2mgAρ

⇒v=8+1.2

⇒v=3.033 m s-1

⇒v≃3 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: The surface tension of a soap bubble is $0.03$ N/m. The work done in increasing the diameter of bubble from $2$ cm to $6$ cm is $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take $\pi = 3.14$)
question: The surface tension of a soap bubble is $0.03$ N/m. The work done in increasing the diameter of bubble from $2$ cm to $6$ cm is $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Take $\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
Initial radius $r_1 = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1$ cm $= 10^{-2}$ m

Final radius $r_2 = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3$ cm $= 3 \times 10^{-2}$ m

Surface tension $T = 0.03$ N/m

A soap bubble has two free surfaces. The change in total surface area is given by:

$\Delta A = 2 \times 4\pi (r_2^2 - r_1^2)$

$\Delta A = 8\pi ((3 \times 10^{-2})^2 - (10^{-2})^2)$

$\Delta A = 8\pi (9 \times 10^{-4} - 10^{-4}) = 64\pi \times 10^{-4}$ m$^2$

Work done $W = T \Delta A$

$W = 0.03 \times 64\pi \times 10^{-4}$

$W = 1.92\pi \times 10^{-4}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \pi \times 10^{-4}$ J, we get $\alpha = 1.92$.

Answer: $1.92$

subject: Physics
q: Air of density $1.2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ is blowing across the horizontal wings of an aeroplane in such a way that its speeds above and below the wings are $150 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $100 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, respectively. The pressure difference between the upper and lower sides of the wings, is :
question: Air of density $1.2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$ is blowing across the horizontal wings of an aeroplane in such a way that its speeds above and below the wings are $150 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $100 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, respectively. The pressure difference between the upper and lower sides of the wings, is :
